mappings.rationaleAI: Core text: covers galaxy classification and distribution (A.1), physical properties (A.2), clusters (A.4), the Milky Way (B), quasars (A.5.c), and galaxy evolution (A.6).

Core text: covers the Hertzsprung-Russell diagram (B.4, D.5.a), stellar evolution (D.7), nuclear fusion and energy generation (D.6.b), variable stars including novae and supernovae (D.4), neutron stars and white dwarfs (D.7).

Covers the physics of galaxies and stars including stellar atmospheres and internal structure (D.6), electromagnetic spectrum observations (D.2.c), magnetic fields (B.5.h, B.6.c), and galactic dynamics (B.6.d).
Relevant to galaxy clusters and their distribution (A.4), quasars (A.5.c), the Milky Way's place in the universe (B), and the origin and evolution of galaxies (A.6).
Relevant as the nearest and best-studied star, providing the observational basis for understanding stellar properties (D.2-D.3), stellar structure (D.6), and magnetic fields (B.5.h).
Directly relevant to quasars and active galactic nuclei (A.5), extragalactic X-ray sources (A.5.b), and end states of stellar evolution including neutron stars (D.7).
Covers the historical development of stellar classification, constellations and star naming (D.1.b-c), and the discovery of the Milky Way's structure (B).
Covers radio and optical telescopes essential for determining galaxy distances (A.1.b), observing extragalactic radio sources (A.5.a), and measuring stellar spectra and luminosities (D.2).
Relevant to stellar spectra (D.2.c), the electromagnetic spectrum used for galaxy and star observation, and the wave-particle duality underlying spectral analysis (D.2.b).
Relevant to gravitational condensation in star formation (D.7.a.i), galactic rotation and dynamics (B.6.d), and interactions between galaxy cluster members (A.4.b).
Relevant to the formation of chemical elements in stars through nucleosynthesis (D.7.b) and the chemical evolution of the Galaxy (B.7.b).
Covers electromagnetic radiation relevant to radio galaxies (A.5.a), X-ray galaxies (A.5.b), and the observational methods used across the electromagnetic spectrum for stellar and galactic study.
Relevant to the formation of planetary systems around stars as part of stellar evolution (D.7) and the broader context of star formation (B.7.c).
Relevant to stellar nucleosynthesis and the formation of isotopes in stars (D.7.b), connecting to the chemical evolution of the Galaxy (B.7.b).
